clean_story:
Wind’s push. Water’s dance.

The phrase "wind sculpts currents" mirrors how a potter shapes clay—except the artist is invisible, and the clay never stops moving. Wind doesn’t just ripple the surface; it drags the top layer of water like a finger skimming milk, pulling deeper layers along through friction. This tug creates spirals: clockwise in the northern hemisphere, counterclockwise in the south, as if the ocean were stirring itself with two giant spoons.

Take the Gulf Stream. Trade winds near the equator shove warm water westward until it hits the Americas, then bounces north—a 100-kilometer-wide river in the Atlantic, moving at 2 meters per second, fast enough to carry a message in a bottle from Florida to Ireland in months. Or watch the Agulhas Current off South Africa, where gusts pinch its tail into whirlpools so tight they trap fish for weeks, turning patches of sea into floating buffets for seabirds.

The precision lies in the angles. Wind hitting at 45 degrees generates the strongest currents; shallower or steeper, and the energy dissipates like a misthrown ball. Satellites now measure these shifts in millimeters—NASA’s SWOT mission maps water heights to predict where winds will next carve a path.

Three related subjects are ocean gyres’ garbage traps, how tides fight wind, and plankton riding current highways.
